A Bantry cooper writes to us:—*“ DEAr Smr,—I beg

they will find that if whiskey and acrated water is their
choice when the magic ** What'll you have *’ is uttered,
they will have to tale their own whiskey to Clery’s,
for, alas, the ““free drinks” only comprise aerated
water. This drapery is now plunging in syphons and
bulbs. It ought to go in for * free bread ”’ next and
run an opposition to Boland’s! Torward, syphons!

It seems rather plain that the 1.A.0.S. are consider-
ing some attempt to wipe out large numbers of small
shopkeepers. The organ of Plunkett House has a long,
dull and very ponderous. article in its issue of August
12th; it is written in the heaviest and most platitudi-
nous style of the spook editor who has apparently
ceased barging at T. W. Russell. The little huxters’
shops in Ireland are too numerous, and in the ordinary
process of commercial evolution their standard will be
raised, just as the very low standard of comfort brought
about by the working out of rapacious and cruel land-
lordism inevitably tends to rise, now that the class
to which Sir Horace Plunkett belonged, and to which
smaller fry of his company were hangers-on, is being
bought out. Tt is certainly rather imitating to find
landlords and ascendancy- dog-boys preaching to the
country, with the whites of their holy eyes turned up,
on the lack of commercial development and other things
amongst a people whom their class and breed with the
help of the British Government had nearly starved and
bled to death. TIf the country has gombeen men, who
are ultimately responsible for them but the landlords?
If you had to pay your rent or go on the roadside, per-
centages of interest would not stop you from borrowing
the money anywhere; and in all communities there are
people like gombeen men and the grasping Irish land-
lords only too ready to squeeze the poor and extort
from the weak. Now the sight of backward farmers
and huxters’ shops hurts the ‘* economising eye-’’ of
ascendancy upstarts and the like.

On desert shores beneath a shed of reeds
Xavier, I see thee breathe thy soul away |
Thy Lord now sendeth thee this long’d-for day
When He would crown thy life and wonderous deeds.
For thy neceds
Perplex us, little men with hearts of clay.
O’er thee thy spirit ruled with rapturous sway,
“Through thee commanded credence from old creeds.
And thou wert like the Indies too, where sweet
Aromas from spice islands load the air!
Thou had’st their kings and nations at thy feet
When, lonely in thy ecstasy of prayer,
The scented breeze bore to the judgment seat
Thy soul more sweet-than Orient incense rave!
